svg.text: When to Use Non-Comparison Sorts
They only work on integer-like keys because they use values as array indices.
Non-comparison sorts CANNOT sort strings, floats, or objects.
They need integer values to use as array indices: count[value] or bucket[value / range].
Works great
Exam scores (0-100)
Counting sort: k = 101 slots. Tiny array, O(n) time.
Student ages (18-65)
Counting sort: k = 48 slots. Trivial memory cost.
Employee IDs (6-digit numbers)
Radix sort: d = 6, k = 10. Six passes of counting sort.
Race finish times in milliseconds
Convert to int (ms), then counting sort. k = range of times.
ZIP codes (5-digit)
Radix sort: d = 5, k = 10. Fast and stable.
Use comparison sort instead
Names / strings
Can't index an array with "Alice". Use quick sort or merge sort.
Floating-point numbers (3.14, 2.71)
Can't use 3.14 as an array index. Use comparison sort.
Objects sorted by a field (Student by GPA)
Need custom comparator. Non-comparison sorts can't do this.
Random 64-bit integers
k = 2^64. Count array won't fit in any computer's memory.
Mixed data types
Non-comparison sorts need uniform integer keys. No mixed types.
Quick Decision Guide
1. Is your data integers (or easily convertible to integers)?
No → use quick sort or merge sort (comparison sort)
2. Is the value range k small relative to n?
Yes (k is small) → counting sort
Uniform distribution? → bucket sort
Large range, few digits? → radix sort
No (k is huge) → use quick sort or merge sort
